title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: When reds are in, dipnets come out FISHING: Multiple factors make for success. By JOSEPH ROBERTIA Peninsula Clarion Published: July 2, 2006 Last Modified: July 19, 2006 at 04:41 PM KENAI -- As millions of sockeye salmon prepare to storm the rivers in an attempt to make the long journey up to their spawning grounds, a seemingly equal number of people are preparing for their own pilgrimage to catch them -- all part of the unique Alaska activity known as dipnetting. "Unique" is almost an understatement for this personal-use fishery, since it is one of the few places Alaska residents can go during summer and know they are among their own. Non-residents -- aka Outsiders -- are not allowed to participate, handle the gear, handle the fish or even drive a boat involved in dipnetting. As opposed to…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Fishing chart Published: June 22, 2006 Last Modified: June 22, 2006 at 03:32 AM SUSITNA VALLEY Willow Creek (4) King salmon, trout, grayling ***** King fishing has been good to excellent, so prepare for crowds. Remember the creek is closed to king fishing above the Parks Highway. However, you can catch rainbow trout and grayling there. Matanuska Valley lakes Trout, grayling, char, landlocked salmon, pike **** Dry-fly possibilities are here for trout and grayling. Fishing is the best in the morning or late evening. Long, Finger and Wiener are fishing well. Talkeetna area lakes Trout, grayling, char **** Looking to duck the crowds? Check out one of the trout or char lakes off the Talkeetna Spur this weekend. title: 1 of 3 A group of climbers with the Mount McKinley Weather Station Project ascend the summit's ridge last month. Annuall…
summary: 1 of 3 A group of climbers with the Mount McKinley Weather Station Project ascend the summit's ridge last month. Annually, Japanese mountaineer Yoshitomi Okura and others climb North America's highest mountain to install a new weather station on a ridge above Denali Pass at 18,700 feet in memory of Okura's three friends who died on the mountain during a winter climb. Photo by TOHRU SAITO / The Associated Press

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Sen. Murkowski pulls in a 63-pound king The Associated Press Published: July 19, 2006 Last Modified: July 19, 2006 at 03:14 AM FAIRBANKS -- Alaska U.S. Sen. Lisa Murkowski had the home river advantage. She caught a 63-pound king salmon in the 11th annual Kenai River Classic, the biggest catch among the nine senators who took part in the annual fundraiser this month for conservation of habitat along the Kenai River. BP Vice President Peggy Hudson caught the largest fish, a 67-pound king. The event raised about $800,000. Alaska's other U.S. senator, Ted Stevens, and Gov. Frank Murkowski co-hosted this year's event. It drew about 200 people, many of them lobbyists and executives from major U.S. industries. Others senators attending this year were Kit Bond of Missouri, Thad Cochran of Mississi…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Climber records the winds on Mount McKinley Japanese climbers' tragedy becomes challenge to measure climate By MARY PEMBERTON The Associated Press Published: July 17, 2006 Last Modified: July 17, 2006 at 01:11 PM Japanese mountaineer Yoshitomi Okura climbs North America's highest mountain each year and installs a new weather station to defend the honor of three friends -- blown off Mount McKinley during a winter climb. An autopsy showed the climbers -- among the 96 people who have died on the peak since the first deaths were recorded in 1932 -- were flash frozen by the ferociously cold winds. Their bodies were recovered only because a rope they used to tie themselves together snagged on ice. "You could see how they were blown down from the high camp. They were just lying there like they we…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Dipnetting guide finds home on Copper River MARK HEM: His recorded fishing report has given him a statewide reputation. By KELLY BOSTIAN Fairbanks Daily News-Miner Published: July 16, 2006 Last Modified: July 16, 2006 at 03:12 AM CHITINA -- During a lighter moment Mark Hem showed just how enthralled he is with the Copper River, even after 23 years of piloting the waterway. Floating in the middle of the churning river one recent Saturday, he plopped his travel coffee mug back into the center of the roll of duct tape he uses as a cup holder on the dash of his custom-built, 24-foot Wooldridge. He cut the power to its 390-horsepower Vortec engine, and as the growl ceased and ears tuned into the silence, Hem grinned. "Have I showed you this one?" The silt-laden river slid under the 7-foot-wide …

summary: Last man standing plods on in unsupported Arctic trek 1,000 KILOMETERS: Three started in Point Hope; only one is left to complete the journey. By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: July 9, 2006 Last Modified: July 9, 2006 at 05:07 AM Click to enlarge Roman Dial Click to enlarge Jason Geck Click to enlarge Ryan Jordan DIAL FINISHES • According to the group's web site, Roman Dial reached the Dalton Highway just after midnight Wednesday, 23 days and 8 hours after starting out. title: Copper reds stall, then flood their way upriver
summary: CHITINA -- What the Alaska Department of Transportation couldn't do, it appears Mother Nature has done: Close Copper River Road to cars and trucks. After a landslide took out a couple hundred yards of the road in June 2001, the Transportation Department ordered the route officially closed, saying there just wasn't enough money to justify stabilizing the one-lane gravel roadway near Chitina used almost exclusively by dipnetters. And though the road was theoretically shut to all traffic -- cars, trucks, all-terrain-vehicles, mountain bikes, even walkers -- the latter three never stopped flowing across the slide as dipnetters scrambled to reach their favorite fishing sites. Meanwhile, enterprising Alaskans with shovels, picks and other tools kept pecking away at the rubble in the slide area u…

title: When reds are in, dipnets come out
summary: KENAI -- As millions of sockeye salmon prepare to storm the rivers in an attempt to make the long journey up to their spawning grounds, a seemingly equal number of people are preparing for their own pilgrimage to catch them -- all part of the unique Alaska activity known as dipnetting. "Unique" is almost an understatement for this personal-use fishery, since it is one of the few places Alaska residents can go during summer and know they are among their own. Non-residents -- aka Outsiders -- are not allowed to participate, handle the gear, handle the fish or even drive a boat involved in dipnetting. As opposed to rod and reel fishing, where countless hours can be exhausted trying to land a single salmon, dipnetters commonly pull in two, three or even four fish at a time while attempting to …

summary: Outdoors hot line SITE OF THE WEEK Published: May 28, 2006 Last Modified: May 28, 2006 at 03:32 AM DERBY STANDINGS (As of May 22) Homer Halibut: 1st: 162.6 lbs., Sandra McPike, Delaware, Ohio, May 21, fishing with Capt. Valdez Halibut Derby is now under way The Valdez Halibut Derby began May 20, and already anglers are out catching big fish. The prize for the largest halibut caught is $15,000. Second place is $5,000 and third place is $2,000. The derby runs through Sept. 3. title: Last man standing plods on in unsupported Arctic trek
summary: DIAL FINISHES • According to the group's web site, Roman Dial reached the Dalton Highway just after midnight Wednesday, 23 days and 8 hours after starting out. In his blog for Thursday he said he would walk a mile on the highway before hitchhiking home. Arctic adventurer Jason Geck is home in Anchorage now appreciating the comforts of civilization. Former Brooks Range companion Ryan Jordan is back at his residence in Bozeman, Mont., nursing the injury that forced him to abandon a planned 1,000-kilometer trek across the top of North America about a third of the way into the journey. And Alaska Pacific University professor Roman Dial struggles on, pushing day by day closer to the Dalton Highway and the end of the muscle-tearing, body-beating, spirit-challenging Arctic 1000. Begun more than t…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Hiker dies following tumble QUESTIONS: Whether man slipped or suffered heart attack before falling is uncertain. By KYLE HOPKINS and CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: July 11, 2006 Last Modified: July 11, 2006 at 02:37 AM On a warm July day beneath a sky full of puffy white clouds, a 75-year-old Anchorage man's hike to the top of Alaska's most-climbed peak took a deadly turn. Authorities said Monday they didn't know if Gene Paul Bryner simply slipped while following a heavily used trail up the northwest side of Flattop, the distinctive peak in the front range of the Chugach Mountains, or if he might have had a heart attack and lost his footing. Bryner fell about noon near the top of the mountain, said Anchorage Fire Department spokesman Tom Kempton. It was not immediately clear …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Annual red return leaps Fisheries experts credit global warming for unexpected salmon numbers By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: July 9, 2006 Last Modified: July 9, 2006 at 07:54 AM Click to enlarge RUSSIAN RIVER -- The red salmon came again this June in a gray-backed wave of life that at times nearly obscured the rocky, rubble bottom of this stream. No one could have imagined such a bounty nearly 40 years ago when the groundwork was being laid for the revitalization of the popular Kenai Peninsula fishery. The river then supported a healthy salmon fishery, but it was nothing compared to what it is today. Red salmon used to return each summer by the thousands or tens of thousands. Now, between the early run just finishing and the late run just beginning, they come by the hundre…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Russian River anglers seeing red SALMON, THAT IS: Fishers are catching their limit quickly. By TONY LEWIS Daily News correspondent Published: June 22, 2006 Last Modified: June 22, 2006 at 03:32 AM COOPER LANDING -- Alex Britton may be the most optimistic fisherman you'll ever meet. For nearly five hours Monday morning Britton flipped flies into the Russian River with nothing to show for it. That's nothing new for the Girdwood resident. He has fished the Kenai Peninsula's famous salmon stream a couple of times before and has yet to land a red salmon. On Monday he arrived at the Russian at 3 a.m. and hiked 30 minutes upstream from the Russian River campground with a couple of buddies. Although he hooked a few salmon, he couldn't bring the hard-fighting fish to the bank. Seasoned anglers, who…

summary: Outdoors hot line SITE OF THE WEEK YOUNG OWLS MAKE DEBUT Published: July 2, 2006 Last Modified: July 2, 2006 at 04:25 AM BIKING Wombats group is planning getaway ride to Kincaid Park The Women's Mountain Bike and Tea Society meets at 7 p.m. Wednesday at the Jodphur trail head to ride sections of Kincaid Park, including such steeper trails as Horseshoe, Icebox and Jodphur. Helmets are required. ( www.wombats.org ) Fireweed 400 race entrants reach a record-high of 700 The Fireweed 400 bike race, which begins Saturday morning from Sheep Mountain Lodge and goes to Valdez and back, has hit a record high 700 entrants.
